topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the elegant script of the first chapter, a familiar comfort against his fingertips. He’d reread it a hundred times, the cadence of the prose a balm to his soul. Then, a jarring sentence snagged his attention—a passage he distinctly remembered crafting for his debut novel. A coldness seeped into his chest, spreading outward like ink on damp paper. He flipped pages frantically, his breath catching in shallow gasps. When he reached the section in question, the words were identical, the thematic progression eerily mirrored. He leaned back in his worn armchair, the springs groaning under his weight, his jaw slackening. "Impossible," he whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet study. He stared at the page, his eyes wide, trying to reconcile the familiar with this unsettling duplication.

[story 2]

"You can't be serious," Maya muttered, the words tasting like ash in her mouth. She held the manuscript in her hands, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy, almost insulting. Every carefully constructed metaphor, every intricate plot twist she’d poured her heart into, was laid bare, reproduced flawlessly in the work of Julian Vance. Her hands began to tremble, the tremor escalating until she had to grip the edge of the desk to steady herself. She dropped the papers onto the mahogany surface with a sharp clatter. A profound stillness descended upon her; her gaze drifted from the offending text to the window, where the afternoon sun seemed suddenly too bright, too harsh. A slow, heavy sigh escaped her lips, a sound of utter deflation.

[story 3]

Liam reread the opening paragraphs, his brow furrowing in intense concentration, searching for a misplaced comma, a subtle deviation. But there was nothing. The narrative flow was seamless, the character motivations perfectly aligned with his own internal landscape. He reread it again, this time slower, his eyes scanning the lines as if expecting a hidden watermark. When he finally reached the passage, a strange pressure built behind his eyes. He closed his book with a soft thud, the sound echoing loudly in the otherwise silent apartment. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of his living room, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. His voice, when he finally spoke, was low and strained, "This isn't right."
